item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some weird stuff I need to point out: "Fiesta mexicana grande" doesn't make sense as the name of a restaurant or even literally. Feels like something you could read in a catalogue of parties provided by a party company. "Yes, we do small Mexican parties and also large Mexican parties." You could be going for "Ignorant US citizens appropriate Mexican culture for a restaurant but don't even bother choosing a thought out name" (God knows there are plenty of those IRL) so it's not a mistake per se.

Also this:

have been retained as final SCP-2848: Final Investigation

Sounds clunky as all hell.

Other than that, it's a middling skip that doesn't stand out nor falls apart. Not all of them can be bangers I guess. I'll upvote because it was well written and the approach is somewhat interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well written, but the logs themselves seem to be a needless expenditure of Foundation resources, near infinite they may be. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice little modernist story told in the SCP style. Simple, surprisingly evocative. For me, the fact that this man was copied into the taco instead of being trapped there or transformed is the little SCP X factor that makes this a winner. It almost feels like it's about these types of stories instead of being one itself; like, for the SCP meat grinder, Gregor Samsa is just another day at the office, and, in some weird way, they appreciate each other for it. The item being Mexican food is also a little reminiscent of magical realism, which is a fun detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm neutral but tilting on an upvote here. The fact that this guy ended up being or at least being mirrored in a taco is barely touched upon beyond the first paragraph of so (eg. why do we assume the entire taco is an entity, if only the tortilla does anything?), and at it's core the arc is yet another "woe is anomaly", though at least not in the overplayed "but containment screws people up, you see?" theme. I really like the half-seen story in the last log, but at the same time I have to wonder: did that need any of the rest of the article? I don't even mean the taco bit, I mean the interviews and the article in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a world as weird as the world of the Foundation, having someone's personality projected onto anything doesn't feel that strange. I am mostly disappointed in how long the article spent telling the readers that they don't get to learn more about this person.

There also seem to be quite a few tense issues in the beginning of the article. Past tense (article was obviously written in this case, remove the update section) or present tense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can understand why people like this, and I can understand why people don't. It's a sentimental piece that shows a more humane side of the SCP, which is nice and heatwarming, but then fails the "Writing A Humanoid SCP" test for me by the researchers not dehumanizing the taco enough. If the aspect of the original man throwing away his emotions was played up, and the terrible nature of a mostly emotive being held by the SCP emphasized I'd upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1'd at

SCP-2848 is a taco

all because I was rolling my eyes at what I thought was going to be a mediocre humanoid (based on the containment protocols) and then it was a taco.

Sure, whatever, it might not be perfect, but I've been lurking and reading this site with abandon for months now and I have never been that surprised by a single sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never thought I'd read a commentary on aging and depression vis-a-vis sentient taco, but here I am. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite its origins, I admired this human side of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this should be tagged 'depression'

"Depression" is not a tag currently used by the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good. It's warm and filled with humane attention to dignity, for once I actually believe that a member of the Foundation wants to help people. I think it's fair to say *this is what SCP-wiki is all about*, surreal anomalous phenomena that are entirely unexplainable but still manage to "speak to the human condition" despite appearing to be silly memeposts on the surface level.

This isn't the story of a sentient taco, it's the story of the [spiritual/cognitive imprint] of a very sad man who is trapped in a taco for some reason…and he's ok with it??? WTF.

I feel like this should be tagged 'depression', I mean seriously how is it possible for someone to be at peace with being a taco? Maybe if he was a fish taco I could understand…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But other people can make good points which after looking at their opinions, I agree with

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it started out dumb. Then I just kept reading and reading, assuming there was going to be something about it that was compelling or clever, and that just never came.

This was a complete waste of my time. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm neutral on this one. The talking taco is so ridiculous, any humor achieved through the dissonance with its story is lost. There's just too much dissonance. The comment above pointing out that this particular story has been done multiple times with other objects is also keeping me from upvoting. Time to do something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Drama or not, this simply failed to interest me and I rolled my eyes at a few of the sappier bits.

its ashes were spread over the beach at Cape Cod.

No. We don't give funerals to anomalies unless it's somehow part of containment, especially when it involves spreading the remains of an anomaly across a public area. Why even bring it to Cape Pod of all places?

-1

item.title: Staff Post - Stop Order
item.content:encoded:
We can discuss how the circumstances of an article should be considered when critiquing it over in this other thread.

Kalinin the user had other issues with the article besides its circumstances, including that they believe the idea is silly and cannot get over that idea enough to upvote- which is totally fair. How much Kalinin's other comments are appropriate-such as the reasons why this article was made at all- should be a matter of discussion that isn't for here.

However, the circumstances of this specific article and how that affects the work in context of it being a mainlist site article is fine. Whether or not the discussion should be had at all when considering the article is for the linked thread and not for this thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the Site Rules: You may vote for any reason you like, so long as your reasons are based solely on the content of the article.

You are grossly misinterpreting this rule.

What the rule says is that reasoning of the type "I don't like Roget (true) and TroyL (false) and since this is a collab between the two of them, that's a double downvote" has no place on this site.

In Kalinin's case the reasoning, however is "The reason for certain writer choices in this article, such as it being a taco exist solely as a thin-veiled fuck you to another user. Since I don't approve of self-serving features like that, and think this article could have had more depth if the subject matter was different in a way that'd intrinsically link with the concept better, that's a double downvote with a side of fries and a large milkshake." And this is perfectly OK.

See, the political content of an article is an intrinsic part of it. Just like you can't reasonably criticize djoric's christian succubus SCP-2166 without noting that it's a screed against SCP-166, you can't criticize this one without ignoring the fact that it's, ultimately, spawned off of a fuck-you aimed at bbaztek. To do otherwise would be blatantly dishonest and ultimately, pointless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Other people's comments and personal agenda shouldn't come into it. Vote based on the article, simple as that. Am I wrong?

Yes. You are. You're vastly oversimplifying it. Unless you want to start reporting people who have come into the comment section on articles and said "hey, after reading [insert username here]'s comments on this, I've changed my mind and am now voting this way because they have a good point." Judgment is a complex process. The content of the article is not always neatly limited to the article, and it does not exist solely in its own little universe, unaffected by outside factors. Articles get downvotes for being too close to other articles. They get downvoted because they're an example of a recently oversaturated cliche. They get downvoted because they hew too closely to other IPs without crossing the plagiarism line.

There is a host of factors to be considered that are not within the literal boundaries of the article itself, because readers are free to use their discernment to judge something in its own native environment, which includes the site as well as the concepts raised by the article's content. This subject is not nearly as cut and dry as you're making it out to be.

I see zero room for debate in that - mine was a direct quote from the Site Rules. You can reject that all day but unless you're right and the Site Rules are wrong, it would be pretty pointless.

You seem to think that your interpretation of the Site Rules is the only possible one. You are mistaken.

So - if someone wants to downvote because it's a poor article, I have zero complaint. If someone wants to downvote became it's come from a particular user or because of behind the scenes politics, then that is completely, totally non-negotiably(?) wrong.

The "behind the scenes politics" that generated this article are a part of it, regardless of varying levels of awareness of them. They affect the content of the article. In my view, that makes that subject fair game.

Again, if you think rules violations are occurring, report them. Since this is non-negotiably wrong and all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Other people's comments and personal agenda shouldn't come into it. Vote based on the article, simple as that. Am I wrong?

I am not going to abandon principles of honest critique because you and "most folks" are uncomfortable with the subject

Eh? Nobody is asking you to, but if someone can't provide honest critique without breaking the Site Rules, someone needs to look at how they critique. I see zero room for debate in that - mine was a direct quote from the Site Rules. You can reject that all day but unless you're right and the Site Rules are wrong, it would be pretty pointless.

Finally, although you appear to have taken personal umbrage, my initial comments were directed at everybody who was sitting on the "I like the article but not the history so I don't know how to vote" fence.

So - if someone wants to downvote because it's a poor article, I have zero complaint. If someone wants to downvote became it's come from a particular user or because of behind the scenes politics, then that is completely, totally non-negotiably(?) wrong.

And that is my argument and the only one I've been trying to make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But feigned ignorance of a topic in order to subtly push a certain platform is so cool, dontcha know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you think the discussion is solely about an animate taco, you need to read the thread again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you think there's been a rules violation, I would prefer that you contact staff about it and let them sort it out, rather than dance around the subject and implying that I'm out of order. I already explained my reasoning thoroughly. I'm not going to restate it if that reasoning is going to be ignored.

TBH I'd rather just come to this site as a place to read good, interesting fiction and leave politics at the door, and I'm sure most folks agree.

Then you need to take that point up with Troy. I am not going to abandon principles of honest critique because you and "most folks" are uncomfortable with the subject. I am not the person who chose to inject that particular piece of site drama into the main list again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the reason he mentioned ""external" hi-jinx" was that he knew about them and could see how they made the article worse than it could be. His vote is because the article isn't very good, but he still refers to the hi-jinx in his criticism as a way to help the author and, I guess, call attention to something he thinks is an issue?

I don't like to make posts that aren't really on-topic (this is an SCP's discussion page, after all!), so I'm going to say that I downvoted this article, for reasons best described by Psul on the previous page :-P

I like the idea that AzureRathalos has below, that if you're writing a talking taco, it has to be really good (because we have a lot of random things that just talk!) and be a story best told through a talking taco. I don't have any ideas for what it should be though, so… :-X

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having read through the comments, there appears to be some drama here? I honestly don't care, i find the article engaging and interesting. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*clicks link* Hey, isn't that a pair of-

…you sneaky devil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based solely on the article - which is how it should be judged

No. I reject this assertion.

From the Site Rules: You may vote for any reason you like, so long as your reasons are based solely on the content of the article.

I accept that whatever the "external" hi-jinx, you also have reasonable in-article concerns to secure a downvote. Not that what anyone else thinks matters to our personal votes, as long as they follow the rules.

TBH I'd rather just come to this site as a place to read good, interesting fiction and leave politics at the door, and I'm sure most folks agree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy whimsy. I also enjoy spats.

This was an easy enough vote for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this discussion even more entertaining than the one about the relationship that was "blatantly heterosexual" and "not gay enough". I might even bookmark this article just so I can come back here and remind myself that yes, people will argue about anything - even a talking taco.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My opinions:

+ show block
– hide block

I don't care either way about the origins, but it tries really hard to tug at the heartstrings and kinda just yanks them. The note at the end read like the good doctor was breaking the fourth wall to beg me not to downvote, and I took it as a challenge I guess.

I actually kind of like the fact that the Foundation is powerless to help this guy. Exploring that more would make me interested in this, maybe in favor of cutting some of the stilted interviews. They seemed hit and miss for me (SCP-2848's first line was too on-the-nose for me, the second interview kind of spoke volumes with its plainness [keep it], while third made me wonder why SCP-2848 didn't at least try to cooperate with the only people trying to help him [depression, I guess… it's accurate but not a compelling narrative]).

Overall I think you made a good effort, but we don't have to rewrite everything. Might be time to put Mr. Sad Taco to pasture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once again, this is spot on.
I'll just add that the last iteration of this, with sapient taco originating from a batch of sapient meat began well, before descending into what I can only describe as random crap. This here, being a taco is an incidental property, and none of the actually interesting aspects, like, how exactly did a taco gain sentience when perused by its putative purchaser, get explored at all.

The moral of the story is that writing an article as a fuck-you or as a take-that is a subtle art where you got to get it right the first time, and if you fail and retry, you're more than likely to just embarrass yourself. I learned this the hard way with the Saddest Necrophile ,and it seems the lesson comes pre-packaged in the form of a taco to some other people now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with both the contention that a sapient taco is "monumentally stupid"

Yep, you can certainly do that. Judging by the votes on this I'd say that there's plenty of supporters on both sides of this question.

and with the contention that this scip is poor only because it was created as a "screw you"

I disagree with your making stuff up, attributing it to me, and then replying to it as though you're somehow having a discussion with me. Did you miss the large swathes of text where I mention my specific problems with the article?

Joe Haldeman's The Forever War was written as a "screw you" aimed at Robert Heinlein's Starship Troopers. Does this reduce its quality as a classic of SF, or invalidate its Nebula, Hugo, and Locus awards?

That would make sense if I had actually made the strawman argument which you attributed to me. Which, you know, I didn't. Here's the important qualifier:

I've levelled this criticism before, where petty site-based agendas were allowed to influence other articles to the detriment of their quality

As it stands, this ain't Hugo Award material. This isn't that discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't a Citizen Kane. Orson Welles didn't kick a a guy out of Hollywood for being an idiot who only thought there could be horror movies ever and then made the best movie of all time because the jerk said "I will look forward to your movie about a sad sled." on his way back to drink himself to death in Kansas or whatever.

The making of Citizen Kane narrative works because Welles is the underdog in that story and because it is the greatest movie of all time. Staff here isn't the underdog in this story and neither version of the sad taco are especially good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess you're not a fan of Citizen Kane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that there's a huge difference between "Starship Troopers draws the wrong conclusions about war, and service" and "this guy was complaining that his 2spoopy4u coldpost got deleted, while this other thing that isn't scary at all was successful, talked himself into being banned and made some parting shot about a sad taco". I think that commenting on weighty issues in response to another monumentally successful work is probably always a good idea, but this other thing is merely petty and taking the bait of a sad angry jerk is probably not worth doing generally ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with both the contention that a sapient taco is "monumentally stupid", and with the contention that this scip is poor only because it was created as a "screw you".

Joe Haldeman's The Forever War was written as a "screw you" aimed at Robert Heinlein's Starship Troopers. Does this reduce its quality as a classic of SF, or invalidate its Nebula, Hugo, and Locus awards?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, we are not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last I checked, wood chippers didn't decay in two weeks in a dry, STP room. So it's close to a WCT, but not quite spot-on :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Are we having another Wood Chipper Test effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having seen the now-departed user's toxic comments and the original Spite Taco post by Roget, I can't rate this with totally clear judgment.

On one hand, I want to see this thing live. I want to see a good writer take this stupid idea from this toxic former user and make a working and interesting article out of it. I want to see something from the incident taken and turned into something good.

On the other hand, the core of this came from a spiteful and stupid idea. Building atop this flawed foundation is a recipe for disaster.

As for the article itself, I think it needs a firm answer to "Why does it have to be a taco?" Make it so that any other object can not take its place in the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this, in its entirely.

Furthermore, the original article that spawned the taco comment (the one with the dancing skeleton guy and his cat; I don't remember the number at this time) probably already proved the point in the first place, so these rebuttals just seem petty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based solely on the article - which is how it should be judged

No. I reject this assertion.

I am not going to rehash the particulars of the incident that led to this idea. Gaffney summarized it in exactly the level of detail it needed. But context matters in certain cases.

You can staple this story onto any non-human object. There is no reason it needs to be a taco, other than to stick it to a particular user. Not only that, but I'm going to go out on a limb and assume that I'm not alone when I say that a sad animate taco is a monumentally stupid concept. Which, bringing us back to the context, was exactly the point in the first place being made by the now-departed user.

I've levelled this criticism before, where petty site-based agendas were allowed to influence other articles to the detriment of their quality. In these cases, instead of writing the best possible piece of fiction they can, the writer gets distracted by their meta-commentary or by trying to prove a point of some sort or whatever. Thus, the outside context actively intrudes upon the quality of the work. That makes this sort of thing fair game when considering judgment of a work. Nothing exists in a vacuum.

This article is a prime example of that. The base story, "guy what's sad and depressed and he dies and we're supposed to be sad," is shoddy. There is nothing to this person's character other than he's depressed. The last addendum is an attempt to provide that character, but really it's nothing more than background data, along with a final entry that's too vague to be anything but cheap mystery-baiting. The mere existence of a depressed person is not enough, in my estimation, to be particularly moving to most readers. So instead of developing that character into something that will make that story affecting, we get this goofy taco business grafted onto it, because by God someone has to prove that they can write an article about a sapient taco. The problems with this article are directly traceable to outside factors. The end result is cynical, shallow feels-mining, hiding behind a gimmick that was beneath a lot of folks here the first time it got trotted out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think there's a presumption that everyone is aware of the real life backstory. I'm not, for one. Based solely on the article - which is how it should be judged - I like it enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
taking other peoples opinions into account

Your opinion is the only one that counts towards your vote.

Just sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There wasn't anything that really made me feel anything in particular…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know nothing of this "spat" of which you speak, nor do I feel it's important - otherwise, I'd have to downvote everything written in response to a challenge. I do vaguely remember the original taco, but I think this reworks the idea rather well. I'm particularly amused by the idea that the GOC got hold of this, decided they had no use for it, and chose to stick "those foolish archivists" with it since it was useless anyway.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I started reading this, I thought, since this was an article about a sapient taco, that it was going to be lighthearted and cute. Then I read it.

my feels
y u do dis troyL

In all seriousness, though, I have to say this one really got to me. The fact that there's no explanation for how 2848 got the way it is, in my opinion, really strengthens it and turns it into this Kafkaesque story that's bolstered by the emotional connection with 2848 that the interview logs manage to establish. And, honestly, I don't really have a problem with the way that the Foundation treats 2848 - after all, they are still human beings, and this is a (presumably) fellow human being who's been caught in a pretty nightmarish situation. I give this a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I don't know anything about the "spat" that is apparently reflecting badly on this skip, but I believe any feelings about the author or previous authors or whatnot should be ignored. This skip either stands on its own, or it doesn't. To me, it definitely does.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

Good:

  • I definitely felt an emotional connection to a sentient taco. Any work that can do that deserves some praise.
  • I think part of the appeal is how good of friends the taco became with the researcher.
  • I think I caught the subtle hook: The Taco isn't M. Tanish, the Taco is M. Tanish' emotional half, only. Or maybe it's only his negative emotions. M. Tanish tried to put these emotions into a taco and throw it away.

Bad:

  • It was a little bit long-winded, and maybe a little too much "tell" in the description and not enough "show" in the logs.

SCP-2848's remains were incinerated in accordance with its final wishes after approval from the O5 Council, and its ashes were spread over the beach at Cape Cod.

That doesn't seem professional. I'd think they'd keep them secured.

It was recovered when the servers, who heard the voices coming from the trashcan and interpreted it as a demon, contacted a local priest for an exorcism, which in turn alerted the Global Occult Coalition, who in turn put SCP-2848 into the Foundation's care for study.

That doesn't seem right. Who immediately jumps to "exorcism" when they see a talking trash can? Maybe radio transmitter? And I don't think the GoC would hand this off to the Foundation, I think they would incinerate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. It's competently written, has some depth to it, but just doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Arghhhhh

This morning I upvoted this just cause I liked it but this isn't amazing. Reading through it again and taking other peoples opinions into account I can't upvote this but I don't think downvoting it is necessary. neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an attempt to rework a fundamentally dumb object written up by a user out of spite in response to a sarcastic, spiteful suggestion by a user on their way out. Echoing psul in wondering why it takes so long to establish something that isn't particularly shocking or interesting.

Also, "SCP-2848 remained unresponsive and did not respond to further inquiries" is redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the psych sessions are supposed to be private, and you wouldn't want other people reading the transcript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a sentient taco is surely interesting/ amusing but there's something about it which makes me not give it a vote. It may be that you're trying a bit too hard to make us sympathize with the taco/ Manfred.

It may be that its treatment by the Foundation is quite relaxed, and I understand the Foundation isn't cruel 'just cause' but… I dunno.

+ A couple of formatting issues
- They're somewhat pedantic but generally minor
  1. Normally changed containment procedures are struck through. Sometimes the revised procedures are above the old ones?
  2. Maybe put the interviews in collapsible?
  3. (Personal Gripe) The 'Final Investigations' table slightly bothers me because of the spacing jump between 'd.'s and the month for the first 3. Don't know if you can do much about it though…

Otherwise I like the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultimately, this reminds me of the talking spider Sorts wrote ages ago, except without any of the emotional impact that one had.

And as much as I'm for well written screw-yous and things done out of spite, I second Kalinin here. This dead horse has received far enough beating as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am really not feeling this one. It feels very disjointed - so much of the set-up seems to be around "how did this happen?" and "who was this taco before?", and the answers end up being "we don't really know" and "You thought it was some old guy, and in fact it was a particular old guy". The interviews (all conducted by Dr Kapera, even though Dr Glass apparently has bi-weekly psych sessions) use a lot of words to confirm (a) his wife is dead; (b) he is estranged from his sons; (c) he is taciturn. All of which is simply confirmed in the Final Investigation.

If a sapient taco is really something that needs to be written, I think this would benefit from a re-write with the taco's human identity being established immediately upon containment. That would remove a huge amount of verbiage that has no great effect, and allows you to concentrate on an old man's thoughts on aging, death and what effect being turned into a taco has on those concepts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I guess we all get to pretend again like this doesn't have its origins in a completely unnecessary personal spat. Allow me to roll my eyes one last time at that.

I suppose one can claim that "animate, sentient taco" is such a compelling story to tell that it transcends the above issues purely by dint of its artistic merit. That's a purely subjective discussion of which I am definitely on the opposite side.

Personally, every time I see one of these kinds of articles, I wonder what we would have gotten if the author had decided to apply the same amount of effort they did into making something good, that happened to start out with some sort of compelling, solid initial concept, instead of an exercise in "oh man I can totally write that you just see if I can't". I am unconvinced that this thing being an animate taco was somehow necessary to the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a rewrite of a failed SCP by Roget. I rewrote the entire concept from the ground up and tried to do a lot of new stuff compared to the old one, so please go in with a open mind.
